glossary.term: data object (also called a data point, an observation, an example)
glossary.definition: a particular unit of data typically represented by a number of feature values
glossary.sentence: A data object can represent one specific patient through the values of patient’s temperature, blood pressure, weight, blood test results, etc.

glossary.definition: a collection of data objects
glossary.sentence: For example, a set of data about patients who attended Hospital A between January and May in 2019.

glossary.definition: a measurable characteristic/property of a data object used as an input variable to a machine learning algorithm
glossary.sentence: For example, the patient’s temperature.

glossary.definition: a specific value of a feature
glossary.sentence: if the feature is “the patient’s temperature”, then its value for a specific patient can be 38oC.

glossary.definition: a set of all features characterising a data object
glossary.sentence: For example, the patient's temperature, blood pressure, weight, blood test results, and others can represent one patient in the dataset.

glossary.definition: an external configuration variable whose value is typically set by the developer arbitrarily before the training
glossary.sentence: For example, learning rate, number of neurons in an artificial neural network, etc.

glossary.definition: a mathematical representation of relationship (mapping) between input and output data of a machine learning algorithm
glossary.sentence: Certain configuration variables describe it (see "A parameter" below).

glossary.definition: a result of applying a model to given data objects
glossary.sentence: A model output - a result of applying a model to given data objects.

glossary.definition: an internal configuration variable of the model whose value is found (learnt) by a machine learning algorithm during the training
glossary.sentence: A parameter (also called a model parameter) - an internal configuration variable of the model whose value is found (learnt) by a machine learning algorithm during the training.

glossary.definition: a subset of the dataset that is used in the testing of the model
glossary.sentence: Testing – the process of checking the performance of the model, usually using data previously unseen by the machine learning algorithm.

glossary.definition: the process of determining the best values of parameters comprising a model and identifying relationships and patterns in the data
glossary.sentence: Training - the process of determining the best values of parameters comprising a model and identifying relationships and patterns in the data.

glossary.definition: carried out when labels of data objects are presented as real values (so they are continuous) as the price of a house, the number of graduating students, etc.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, for a new data object, the machine learning algorithm finds a prediction, for example, the prediction of the possible price of a house, the prediction of the potential number of graduating students, etc.

glossary.definition: deals with segmenting data into representative classes called clusters (Jones, 2008; Pant, 2019).

glossary.definition: finds rules that are based on common co-occurring values of data objects (Pant, 2019), for example, running an association rule analysis on your sales logs may reveal that people who purchase barbecue sauce and potato chips also tend to buy steak (Geron, 2017).
glossary.sentence: For example, say you have a lot of data about your blog’s visitors. You may want to run a clustering algorithm to try to detect groups of similar visitors.

glossary.definition: is associated with finding the most essential features to reduce the original feature set, especially by dropping less important features or merging correlated features (Geron, 2017; Pant, 2019).
glossary.sentence: For example, a car's mileage may be significantly correlated with its age, so the dimensionality reduction algorithm will merge them into one feature that represents the car’s wear and tear (Geron, 2017).

glossary.definition: the data processing that leads to a specific output: in a classification task – a class label, in a regression task - a prediction of the value of a specified property of a new data object.
glossary.sentence: According to (Tamir, 2020), a supervised machine learning algorithm includes three components : a decision process: the data processing that leads to a specific output: in a classification task – a class label, in a regression task - a prediction of the value of a specified property of a new data object; an error function: measuring how good the output is by comparing it to known examples; an updating or optimisation process: reducing the classification or prediction error by updating variables of the mathematical model used by the algorithm.

glossary.definition: a measure used to determine the similarity of data objects.
glossary.sentence: A similarity metric is used such as the distance between data objects because similar data objects are close to each other.

glossary.definition: the measure of difference between data objects.
glossary.sentence: Similar data objects are close to each other. The nearest neighbours are identified for two new data objects based on distance and one data object receives a label of sun, another – of cloud.

glossary.definition: a metric that calculates the square root of the sum of the squares of the differences between the value of the i- th feature of a new data object and the value of the i- th feature of an existing data object in the dataset.
glossary.sentence: The Euclidean distance can be the right choice for measuring the similarity of data objects (Jones, 2009; Kubat, 2017):

glossary.definition: a metric that calculates the sum of absolute differences between feature values.
glossary.sentence: Another distance metrics for continuous feature values is the Manhattan distance :

glossary.definition: a metric that calculates the number of features on which two data objects differ.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, the fewer the differences in features, the greater the similarity of data objects. If the values of the same feature of both data objects are the same, then the distance is equal to 0; otherwise it is equal to 1.

glossary.definition: the description of data objects based on their features.
glossary.sentence: The concept of similarity (sometimes called distance, proximity, or closeness) makes the basis for the algorithm. In this case, the similarity of data objects is determined based on feature-based descriptions of data objects (the so-called feature similarity) (Kubat, 2017).

glossary.definition: a value that defines the number of nearest data objects that will be considered to identify the output value for a new data object.
glossary.sentence: It defines the number of nearest data objects that will be considered to identify the output value for a new data object. The developer sets a value of the hyperparameter before the operation of the algorithm.

glossary.definition: a process where multiple data objects vote to determine the class label for a new data object.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm applies a voting mechanism ; that is, after finding distances of data objects in the dataset to a new data object and sorting data objects from the dataset in ascending order of the calculated distances, the algorithm selects k data objects with smaller distances and take the plurality vote of the neighbours (which is the majority vote in the case of binary classification) (Russell & Norvig, 2010).

glossary.definition: a version of kNN algorithm that finds a single closet data object in the dataset.
glossary.sentence: As a result, in the classification task, the algorithm takes the same class that the closest data object has. However, deciding the output value for a new data object based only on one closest data object leads to unstable decision boundaries.

glossary.definition: a version of kNN algorithm that considers multiple nearest data objects.
glossary.sentence: The algorithm applies a voting mechanism ; that is, after finding distances of data objects in the dataset to a new data object and sorting data objects from the dataset in ascending order of the calculated distances, the algorithm selects k data objects with smaller distances and take the plurality vote of the neighbours (which is the majority vote in the case of binary classification) (Russell & Norvig, 2010).

glossary.definition: data that contains both continuous and categorical features.

As quite usually datasets include mixed-type data – both continuous and categorical, to cope with these data, we can rely on the sum of the squared distances along corresponding features (Kubat, 2017):

glossary.definition: a whole number that is not divisible by two.
glossary.sentence: If the problem is binary classification (there are only two classes), then k should be an odd number to prevent ties (Kubat, 2017; Russell & Norvig, 2010);

glossary.definition: a situation where two or more entities have the same value or rank.
glossary.sentence: If k is six, then three of the selected data objects belong to one class and three to another class. In this case, it is not possible to define a class for a new data object.

glossary.definition: A process where clusters are recursively merged into a single cluster at each level, starting with individual data objects and ending with one cluster containing all data objects. The steps involve merging pairs of clusters with the smallest intergroup dissimilarity until only one cluster remains.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, a pair of clusters is merged at each hierarchy level until one cluster with all data objects is acquired at the highest level.

glossary.definition: A top-down clustering approach that starts with a single cluster containing all data objects and recursively splits it into two new clusters at each hierarchy level, choosing the split to maximize between-group dissimilarity.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, it starts with a cluster with all data objects inside it. Then, at each hierarchy level, it recursively splits one of the existing clusters into two new clusters.

glossary.definition: The measure used to determine the similarity between clusters during merging in agglomerative hierarchical clustering.
glossary.sentence: The pairs chosen for merging consist of the two groups with the smallest intergroup dissimilarity (the fragment is based on the information given in (Hastie, 2017)) .

glossary.definition: The measure used to determine the similarity between clusters during splitting in divisive hierarchical clustering.
glossary.sentence: Therefore, it starts with a cluster with all data objects inside it. Then, at each hierarchy level, it recursively splits one of the existing clusters into two new clusters. The split is chosen to produce two new groups with the largest between-group dissimilarity (the fragment is based on the information given in (Hastie, 2017)) .

glossary.definition: A method used to measure the similarity (distance) between clusters to decide the rules for merging clusters.
glossary.sentence: There are several methods to measure the similarity (distance) between clusters to decide the rules for merging clusters, and they are often called linkage methods .

glossary.definition: A linkage method that calculates the distance between two clusters as the longest distance between any two data objects in the clusters.
glossary.sentence: In deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the clusters’ most distant elements (the longest distance) is calculated: where G, H – clusters; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i' – a data object from the cluster H.

glossary.definition: A linkage method that calculates the distance between two clusters as the shortest distance between any two data objects in the clusters.
glossary.sentence: In deciding about the cluster similarity, the distance between the closest elements of the two clusters (the shortest distance) is calculated: where G, H – clusters; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i' – a data object from the cluster H.

glossary.definition: A linkage method that calculates the distance between two clusters as the average distance between all pairs of data objects, one from each cluster.
glossary.sentence: The distance between two clusters is defined as the average distance between each data object in one cluster to every data object in the other cluster: where G, H – clusters; NG – the number of data objects in the cluster G; NH – the number of data objects in the cluster H; d – distance; i – a data object from the cluster G; i’ – a data object from the cluster H.

glossary.definition: A tree-like diagram representing hierarchical relationships between data objects in the dataset, showing merging of data objects into clusters. It provides a highly interpretable complete description of the hierarchical clustering in a graphical format.
glossary.sentence: A dendrogram consists of (Figure 5): clades that represent merging of data objects into clusters; each clade has its height; leaves that are terminal of clades corresponding to the data objects used in the clustering process.

glossary.definition: A branch or subtree in a dendrogram representing the merging of data objects into a cluster at a specific height.

glossary.definition: An extension to the slot-value structure of frames as they represent extended knowledge about slot values and tell the inference engine how to process the property of the object, allow to set a slot value and control user's access to the slot value ( Negnevitsky , 2011).
glossary.sentence: Facets can contain value type, value constraints (maximum value, minimum value, range of values, etc.), and actions that can be performed with slot values.

glossary.definition: Collections of related frames are linked together into frame-systems ( Minsky, 1975).
glossary.sentence: Thus, to create a knowledge base for the frame-based expert system, we need to define and interrelate frames.

glossary.definition: It contains common properties of a set/class/kind/type of objects/concepts. The class frames help us to represent a problem in an abstract form ( Negnevitsky , 2011).
glossary.sentence: Typically, a class frame includes a title of the class/set/type/kind, a set of common properties, and values of properties that are common to all objects of the class/set/type/kind (Figure 4).

glossary.definition: It can contain new slots that are not included in the class frames but characterize objects belonging to the sub-class.
glossary.sentence: As a result, the class frames allow creating multi-level hierarchies of class and sub-class frames.

glossary.definition: It represents a detailed knowledge about a particular object in the problem domain (Figure 6).
glossary.sentence: Typically the instance frames include all slot values and are derived from the class/sub-class frames.

glossary.definition: A unique title of one frame is used as a slot value in another frame (Figure 7).
glossary.sentence: Therefore, frames are related only by slot values, not by slot titles , because slot titles explain what kind of slot values is stored in the frame.

glossary.definition: It relates a sub-class frame with a class frame or an instance frame with a sub-class/class frame. It allows making hierarchies of frames where the most general frame is located at the highest level and the most specific ones – at the lower levels.
glossary.sentence: In different shells for creating frame-based knowledge bases, this type of relationship can be represented by different slots, like “Is-a”, “a-kind-of”, “class”, and others.

glossary.definition: It allows representing extended knowledge about parts of objects/concepts (Figure 9). In other words, frames corresponding to the components of an object are associated with a frame representing the object as a whole .
glossary.sentence: In different shells for creating frame-based knowledge bases, this type of relationship can be represented by slot titles “a-part-of” or “part-whole”.

glossary.definition: It allows defining any other type of relations by using the referencing principle (Figure 10).
glossary.sentence: Inheritance is an essential feature of frame-based expert systems ( Negnevitsky , 2011).

glossary.definition: A regular procedure is a self-contained piece of computer code written by the developer of an intelligent computer system ( Negnevitsky , 2011).
glossary.sentence: We can use such procedures for different purposes like generating an image of the object, calculating some numerical values, such as the amount of taxes to be paid, acquiring data from external sources, etc.

glossary.definition: An action in a frame-based expert system (Jones, 2009; Luger,2009; Mallach, 1994; Negnevitsky , 2011; Winston, 1992).
glossary.sentence: Demons implement actions in a frame-based expert system.

glossary.definition: An inference engine used when a domain expert first gathers information and then infers from it.
glossary.sentence: If a domain expert first needs to gather some information and then tries to infer from it whatever can be inferred, choose the forward chaining inference engine.

glossary.definition: An inference engine used when a domain expert begins with a hypothetical solution and attempts to find facts to prove it.
glossary.sentence: However, if your domain expert begins with a hypothetical solution and then attempts to find facts to prove it, choose the backward chaining inference engine.

glossary.definition: The set of enabled rules from which one rule is chosen to fire.
glossary.sentence: Conflict resolution is the method of choosing one rule to fire from those that are able to fire, i.e., from the set of enabled rules, known as the conflict set.

glossary.definition: Rules defined with an ‘if... then...” structure used in knowledge representation.
glossary.sentence: Production rules are a natural way of knowledge representation because human experts very often explain problem-solving using constructions like “if... then...”.

glossary.definition: The area where data is entered into the system.
glossary.sentence: This method relies on time tags attached to each fact in the working memory; thus, in the conflict set, the expert system first fires the rule whose antecedent uses the data most recently added to the working memory.

glossary.definition: Used to represent and reason with incomplete and uncertain knowledge.
glossary.sentence: Most rule-based expert systems are capable of representing and reasoning with incomplete and uncertain knowledge by adding confidence factors.

glossary.definition: The iterative cycle used in the execution of production rules.
glossary.sentence: At each stage of the control cycle 'Recognize-act', the selected rule can be displayed.
